GURRENT AFFAIRS.

The autocrat of all the Post Offices, the ‘“ Liberal >’
"~ Minister, Mr. Sydney Buxton, has committed another
dump into Ireland. Our readers may remember that
we drew attention to the fact recently that the Post-
mastership of Castlerea was vacant. The salary is
stated to be £180 per annum with an allowance of £76
against “ Head Office rent.””  This vacancy, we now
learn, has been filled by a dump from England, one
Mr. W. H. Cooper, described as ‘‘ Salaried Sub-post-
master of Ilkeston, Nottingham.”” We are unable to
state the emolument attached to this post in England;
perhaps Mr. William TField would make enquiries.
There is hardly a doubt but that the dump has made
an advantageous change from the money point of view.
The autocrat of all the Post Offices has in this case, no
doubt, as in every other case, selected, according to his
own showing, “ the hest qualified "’ ! :

We also find that one Mr. T. H. Spraggon, who

~ some years ago was dumped into s beil
repatristed. He is leaving £480 a year and Limericl
* hehind him, and is going back to Britain to take up the
~ duties of Postmaster at Sunderland at a salary of £500

22nd JUNE, 1907. One Penny.

o Limerick, is being '

a year. We wonder what chance has an TIrish official
of being promoted to this vacancy thus created in
Limerick.  No doubt with that nice discrimination
which characterises the autocrat of all the Post Offices
in these matters, he will select ‘‘ the best qualified,’
and the best qualified will be a dump from Britain.

The National Commissioners of Education in Ireland
are to be congratulated on the accession of General Sic
William Butler to their ranks. In General Butler the
interests of Trish Ireland will have a friend of a tough
order, who may be trusted to put up a good fight for
his views when necessary. We note with satisfaction
that the Commissioners will proceed at an early date
to appoint six organisers of Irish Language Instruction.
Particulars concerning the proposed appointments will
be found in our advertising columns of last week’s
issue. Candidates must send in their applications not
later than 30th June.

The ‘‘ International ”’ Exhibition continues to do
harm to many traders in Dublin; we have heard that
some traders are actually cursing the show. The
problem is to find where even its alleged advantages
come in, or are supposed to come in. The Irish manu-
facturers, or many of then, we take it, have gone in in
self-defence, and for the country’s sake we would wish
that all the visitors paid special attention to the Irish
exhibits. We did not see any trace of the Tobacco
Combine there, but they may be stowed away in some
corner for aught we know to the contrary. Goodbody’s
have a fine working exhibit; they give an exhibition of
cigar-making, and perhaps some of the visitors from
Cuba, should there be any such, may pick up a
“ wrinkle ’’ or two in cigar-making from the Irish firm.
So far as we have seen, no Cuban firm is showing how
they make cigars in Cuba, so that it appears to be ali-
giving information and no receiving it so far as the
cigar-making industry in Ireland is concerned. Good-
body’s show is indeed a credit to an Irish firm; and the
same may be’ said of Gallagher’s of Belfast, and of
Murray’s of Belfast, each of these firms runs a working
exhibit.

We referred last week to the Canadian Emigration
Pavilion. This week we might say a word or two on
the Somali Village, or alleged village. First of all we
would like to enter our strong protest against the exhi-
bition of people performing their religious worship as
part of the value for the sixpence paid to enter a side-
show. The worship of God in any form is sacred, and
is not a fit subject for a ‘‘ turn’ even at a show at
THumphrey’s Dump. This particular item at this side-
show is a disgrace. If the Somalians, or alleged Soma-
lians, are sincere, it is an outrage on these poor people
that they should be asked to earn money for whoever
owns this side-show, by placing their worship of God on
exhibition for the amusement of Rathmines Johnnies
and frequenters of music halls; if the exhibition of re-
ligion is only a farce, it is none the less objectionable;
rather it appears to be more blasphemous. One native was
ongaged in making sandals ; evidently the Somalians are
more advanced in one respect than the modern Irish,
for they, apparently, are able to shoe themselves; that;
is more than the people of this country can say for
themselyes. Amnother of the natives was making pot-
tory, singing in his traditional style as he worked.
Certainly the potter and the shoe, or sandal, maker
provided lessons to the green people who pay sixpence
to look at them as curiosities! If the sandal-making
African asked the green patriot where his (the patriot’s)
shoes were made, the green one might turn red.

The manifesto of the Irish Parliamentary Party is
a rather weak and wnconvincing dooument. ~ The Irish

- Party were up to their necks in the instalment system.
We say nothing against them for that, because we think
they were sensible in being willing to take an instal-
ment; our chief regret is that they bungled so badly
that they did not succeed in gotting one. Who, by the
way, are the ‘‘ certain officials in Ireland ’’ alluded to
in the manifesto? Vague hints of this kind are not
good enough; let us hear the names of these ‘ certain
officials in Ireland.”

As to the rejection of the Bill making anything mani-
fest to “all the world,” as the manifesto says it does,
we need only point out that ‘“all the world”’ has its
own business to mind without troubling about this
country. Tt is easy to talk of a ‘‘vigorous and well-
sustained agitation,” but where is it to come from, and
what are we to agitate for, and to what end? As we
see it the specialised politicians have sadly failed, and
the idea of preparing for a new frontal political attack
on a grand scale appears to us as only fondling a delu-
sion. Where is the force to come from? How is the
British Government to be ‘coerced ’’—we take the
word from the manifesto—into introducing a Bill “ very
different > from the last ; and if the new Bill, brought in
as the result of Irish coercion of England, were merely
““very different ’’ from the rejected one, and were not
a full measure of Home Rule, 1t would only represent an
instalment ! The Irish struggle, we are told, has entered
on “a new and final phase.”’” What is final about it?
If the next ‘‘vigorous and well-sustained agitation ’’
fails, are we to throw up our hands? Well, whatever
the Irish Party might do in such a contingency, we
won’t throw up our hands. We don’t see any ‘ final
phase ”’ in the present situation. However, we won’t
pursue the manifesto any further. To-day (Thursday)
the Directory of the U.I.L.is to sit on the situation, and
its proposals may be more tangible; let us hope they
will be more satisfactory.

There is another way of getting instalments of Home
Rule besides the system that has failed so ignominiously
—namely, getting one thing at a time rather than a bit
of everything or of many things all at once A satisfac-

“tory settlement of the University problem is an instal-
ment worth having, and we think the Parliamentary
Party would be well advised if they laid themselves out
to secure a University settlement next session. At the
same time the promotion of a movement on the over-
taxation ‘question could be gone on with.

Politics are certainly very much of a mix-up at pre-
sent. The followers of Mr. Martyn in Tuam appear to
be rather strong Parliamentarians! Mr. James Daly
presided at a recent - meeting of the Tuam
Sinn' Fein branch of the National Council. Mr.
Daly made a speech, and wanted to know
why the Kynoch affair was mnot brought before
Parliament when the seizures of the magazines were
made. That is a curious question for Mr. Daly to ask,
for his Party say that Nationalists have no business
going ‘fo Parliament at all. Tn the course of his speech,
Mr. Daly said :—“ Now, fellow-workmen, I say here
that it is' about time that the Irish people cried  halt’
to this renegade Government, and that the Trish Party

- should be called upon to infuse into the people the spirit
which animated Parnell and Davitt. Tt is not by in-
triguing that Ireland will get justice, but by ‘giving the
_English'and Scotchmen a few good stunning blows, and

therefore the country should be aroused and the hills
and valleys scoured by the Irish Party to bring this
miserable Government to their senses.’’ The Irish Party
scouring the hills and valleys is not bad. But how does
Mr. Daly propose that Englishmen and Scotchmen are
to be made recipients of ‘ a few stunning blows.” Per-
haps whilst the Irish Party are scouring the hills and
valleys Mr. Daly and his friends would run the English-
men and Scotchmen through a mangle. The following
rosolution was declared carried unanimously :—‘ Thaf
we, the Tuam Sinn Fein Branch of the National Council,
protest most strongly against the action of the Home
Secretary towards the Arklow factory, and we call upon
the Trish members, and especially the Nationalist mem-
bers, to stand up in their places in the THouse of Cpm«
mons and demand the withdrawal of the prosecution,

and that in the event of the Home Secretary refusing

to do so, we say it is the duty of the Irish Party to
embarrass the Government by every means in their
power, and that if they are not prepared to do so then
they should return to Ireland and make the Govern-
ment of the country impossible for them.”” Evidently
the Tuam Sinn Fein Branch of the National Council is
composed of rather hot Parliamentarians, and it is only
in the event of their not embarrassing the Government
“on the floor ” that they are to come home to make
Government impossible on the spot! As we have
said, politics in Ireland are certainly rather a mix-up
just at present.

The summer courses at the School of Trish Learning,
35 Dawson Street, Dublin, commence on July 1st, under
the direction of Mr. Osborne J. Bergin, Ph.D. Dr.
Bergin will lecture on old Irish, Middle Irish Texts, and
Historical Modern TIrish Grammar.

The Connacht Teis comes off in St. Jarlath’s College,
Tuam, on the 16th, 17th and 18th of July There will
be an Tndustrial Exhibition held in connection with it.
Intending competitors in the literary sections had better
hurry up, as entries close on June 24th—for other com-
petitions the entries close on July 1st. There are £200
offered in prizes at the Connacht Feis. Exhibits for
gl‘;cl industrial sections must reach Tuam before July

3th.

A gentleman called at our offices a few days ago, and
he threw a side-light on the harvestmen on their way
to the British agricultural labour markets. He happened
to be down at the North Wall when there were about
3,000 harvesters awaiting the departure of the boat.
They arrived in Dublin by rail about five hours before
the boat started. Apparently there was no one to look
after them, and the North Wall is not one of the most
select parts of Dublin. We are not surprised to hear
that several of the men were the worse for drink before
the boat left the North Wall, and one unfortunate man
—whether he was drunk or sober we cannot say—found
his way into the Liffey. We believe that, as a conse-
quence, he spent the night in hospital, and not on the
boat. ‘Why is there not a committee or an organisation
to lend a ‘hand to these harvestmen during the hours
they spend in Dublin? If the railway landed them just
in time to catch the boat the dangers attending their
time in the vicinity of the public-houses of the North
Wall would ‘be lessened. If a Protestant organisation,
or an organisation composed of Protestants, looked after
thege men, and made an attempt to provide food and
temperance drinks for them, we would all probably cry
prostlytism ; but why do not the Catholics look after

‘them? Heaven knows, there are enough Catholic women
in Dublin with nothing to do who need not be thanked
if they attempted to justify their existence by engaging
n practical work of this kind. Women are to be seen
every day with hockey clubs and tennis bats and golf
sticks parading round Dublin and its suburbs, but do
they do anything: to justify their existence? There is
- any amount of work crying aloud to the women' of
leisure in Dublin; but how many of them answer the
_call?  We felt humiliated a few weeks ago whilst a
reader of ours from South Africa, who was on a visit to
Ireland, called in to see us, and spoke of the awful
and heartrending sights that the slum streets of Dublin
present. What are the Catholic women of leisure doing?
= Do they think they justify their existence by playing

hockey and flirting with a few Rathmines Johnnies,
who, though eligible, can’t marry on their  fifteen
shillings a week ?

This is only a side-light on the migratory agricultural
labourers. A good deal of more direct light is thrown
on the men and their work by the report of Mr. Adams
on the Irish migratory agricultural labourers,
1906. The last sentence of the report is—‘‘ But
from information obtained regarding the various
groups, the wages and length of employment,
the cost: of living, and the expenditure incurred
in journeying and search for work, it may be estimated
that the savings of the migratory labourers remitted or
brought back to Ireland amount to approximately
£275,000 in the past season.” This is almost a startling
calculation ; the harvestmen represent a net gain to Ire-

land of upwards of a quarter of a million sterling in the
year.

The number of migratory labourers to England ari
Scotland in 1906 is estimated approximately at 25,000,
about one thousand of whom were women. ; We read in
the report:—‘ The question as to what the migratory
labourers can earn in England and Scotland is one of
“practical interest to the Irish farmer to-day, in view of
the competition for efficient agricultural labour. From
the foregoing account it will be seen that, excepting
the Achill workers, the earnings of Irish migratory
labourers vary considerably in different districts and at
different seasons of the year. But the range of wages is
from a lower limit of 15s. a week, with lodging and some
food, to piece-work earnings, which at the busy seasons
in several districts reach 30s., and in some cases even
£2 a week. As a general average, the Donegal and Con-
naught men may be said to make earnings of about £1
a week. But the length of the season also varies widely.
Most of the workers remain about five months, others,
however, as long as eight and nine months.””

We have received the prospectus of McAweeney’s
Commercial School which will be found interest-
_ ing to intending students. We note that at recent
shorthand  speed examinations a lady pupil
secured a certificate for the high rate of 180 words per
minute. Atthe same examinations several other pupils
obtained certificates for speeds varying from 100 to 160
words per minute. The prospectus may be obtained on
application to Mr. T. J. McAweency, 108° Stephen’s
Green, West, Dublin.

to Lismore in May last. Whether his reverence took
any action in the matter I don’t know.”” Perhaps the
respected P.P. of Lismore would let some further light
on how affairs are worked at Lismore Castle.  How
many imported pensioners have been dumped there in
rocent times? The flagrant case of exclusive dealing by
the agent of the Duke of Devonshire’s Lismore
Estate, upon which we turned the light last week,
should not be let rest where it is. Has the Duke of
Devonshire any explanation to give for this explicit boy-
cotting, not only of Catholics, but even of Catholics
who have served in the British army? Have the Dub-,
lin Castle military authorities any explanation to give
for employing the machinery of the Royal Engineers’
staff at Dublin Castle in order to carry out the boycot-
ting and exclusive dealing behests of Mr. L. M. Fitz-
gerald of the Lismore Castle Estate Office.

The Ulster Yeomanry are going in a few days, or by
this time they may have gone, to the Curragh Camp for
a course of training. In the ranks of these gallant and
blood-thirsty sons of Mars a Nationalist and Catholic
medical doctor of Dungannon sends his son. We be-
lieve he is the only Catholic, or Cawtholic, in this fiery
battalion of heroes from Ulster.

We have received the following letter :—‘‘ Dear Sir,
—I am an Irish speaker who am forced to go to the
land of the Saxon in order to make a living. I have
passed a Civil Service exam., and may be soon called
either to London or Edinburgh. I don’t know anyone
in either place and would be glad, through the medium
of your valuable paper, to maks the acquaintance of some
good Irishmen or to learn if there is any Irish associa-
tion which takes an interest in young fellows going over
to English cities. I am afraid there is no such associa-
tion, for I have known boys to go over and fall into
company which ruined them, and hence my anxiety to
put myself into friendly Irish hands,—Mipe, te meap
mop, Pdopais.”  Should our correspondent go to
London, his best course would be to at once get in touch
with the Gaelic League Organisation there. We cannot
say, off hand, if there is any branch of the Gaelic
League in Edinburgh.

We have received the schedule of prizes offered by
the Queen’s County Agricultural Society to be competed
for at the Society’s Annual Show, which will be held
at Maryborough on July 3Ist. The competitions
cover a large variety of classes of live stock and
dairy produce, including poultry, bread, honey, vege-
tables, farming and dairy machinery, lace and crochet
work, etc. The prizes in the Irish lace and crochet section
are presented by the Royal Trish Industries Association,
Dublin, out of the Blanchardiere Bequest, about which
we should like to say a word or two for the information
of our readers who take an interest in lace and kindred
work. The endowment was left for the promotion of
the latter branch of Ivish industry, and the annual in-
come arising from it has, with that object, been placed
in the hands of the Industries Association, 76 Grafton
St., Dublin. The fund was originally devoted as prizes
to the industrial department of the Dublin Horse Show,
bub recently the Association decided (and wisely, we
think) to get more in touch with local industry by dis-
tributing it to various shows to be selected in each county
by the local Committee of Agriculture and Technical
TInstruction. This decentralising system has, we are glad
to learn, met with much success, only three counties out
of the thirty-two not having taken advantage of it. We

““ Dympna Tyrconnell ”’ is the title of a new story
reader for Irish National Schools, issued by Blackic and
Son, Ltd., Dublin. The reader, which is intended for
senior standards, is adapted from Rosa Mulholland’s
“Banshee Castle.’”” Mr. II. P. Courtnoy, already a
valuable contributor to school literature, has edited the
new volume.

We have received a copy of the ¢ Castleknock
Chronicle ** for 1907. There is no contribution in Trish
in it, and by this omission the ‘‘Castleknock Chronicle’’
shows that it is holding out against the Irish-Treland
sentiment with even greater stubbornness than the
 Clongownian.”” On December 8th, there was a con-
cert and a farce entitled ‘‘Ici on parle Francais.”’
French is quite respectable, notwithstanding the state
of the Church in that unhappy realm. On St. Patrick’s
night there was a song in Irish on the programme, and
the entertainment wound up with the chorus ‘‘ To
Maud &y nJaevils Stan.” There is, at least, onc
interesting article in the  Chronicle ’—*‘ The Soul of a
Great School,”” by Father J. S. Sheehy, C.M. One
paragraph of that article is as follows:— ‘T trust at
this critical period in the history of Ireland, it may b
given to Castlekuock to play a great part in the build-
ing of the Irish Nation. There is now arising an Irish
Ireland, which, while trying to learn from the scholar-
ship of other lands, aims especially at the attainment
of ideals dear to Irishmen and Catholics. It is aiming
at the revival of the Irish Language as a spoken tongue,
ay creating an interest in Irish music and Irish dancing,
al, fostering Irish Industries and Art. In the last century
there was a slackening of interest all over the land in
all these matters. But it is the aim of the new century
to change all that. The colleges of the counfry will
simply carry out the wishes of the people. So Castle-
knock will do its duty in this matter. She was as
National as any other college in Ireland in the past.

rightful place, cherishing ideals truly -Irish and

Wo are quite sure that if Father Sheehy had his way
—unfortunately he is not connected with the college—
Castleknock would run the great college at St. Col-.
man’s, Fermoy, close in the matter of Irish spirit.
Tather Sheehy says that :—‘ The colleges of the country
will simply carry out the wishes of the people.”  We
should have thought that the colleges should anticipate
the better wishes of the people, that they should lead
and bo pioneers in every good work concerning faith
and fatherland.  The ‘‘wishes of the people’” in
flabby Ireland is more or less a phrase without a mean-
ing, for the people have been so mis-educated and are
so backboneless that they have no definite wishes; they
don’t know their own minds. If Castleknock will be
“‘again in the van in her rightful place, cherishing ideals
truly Irish and Catholic,”” we would, at least, beg leave
to say that she had better hurry up and make Father
Sheehy, or some man of his stamp, President.

In the “ News of our Past,”” we read:—‘‘ Jack Mor-
rogh has been gazetted Lieutenant of his regiment now
quartered in India. We know how slow is the promo-
tion of Catholics in the army, and we congratulate our
young Pastman on getting to the front so soon.””

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

THE GLASNEVIN AGRICULTURAL FARM.

7Y HERE was a time when the students at the Model
Farm at Glasnevin went in by the back door.

That was under the old regime before the Department
took it over. It was by way, we suppose, of humbly
tipping their hands to their hats at the great institu-
tion. That tradition, like unqualified landlordism, has
gone, and, needless to say, it is a good riddance. The
students play hurling now if they like, and some of
them do like, and so there is a hurling club.  The
back-door tradition is wiped out.  The students are
given plenty of room to grow sturdy characters as well
as sturdy fruit trees; they have their club room, or
rooms, and through various committees composed from
their own numbers they ‘practise self-government and
the art of management. The resident students dine
together in a fine airy refectory, and the dormitories
remind one of College days and pillow encounters; but
pillow fights are a juvenile form of relaxation, and the
students at Glasnevin Farm are young men, or men
getting on for middle age. Someone took exception at
the expense alleged to have been lavished on a
luxurious lavatory; well, no doubt, there are people
who still may think that the students should come in
by the back door; we saw the lavatory, but we could
see no trace of extravagance about it; it is what
one would expect to find in any well-appointed College.
It is often alleged against us that we take more
interest in other people’s business than our own, yet
the fact is that half the people in this country do not
know what the other half are doing. How many of
us know what the Department is doing beyond that
its first vice-president wrote a much-discussed book.
It is only quite recently, notwithstanding all our
gossip, that the Dark Brotherhood has been discovered,

We saw fourteen young men studying practical
igotany; fthey were inquiring at the time we were there
into the mysteries of the stem of grass; we inspected,
not with the eye of an expert, but merely as a mem-
ber of the ““man in the street’’ class, so far as agri-
cultural matters go, the elaborate cattle byres and
poultry runs. The pig styes were most respectable,
and ten little pigs all in a row, ten days old, appealed
to one as irresistibly as ten little Trish terrier pups-—
one’s admiration would cool, no doubt, as the pigs
grew to the years of grunting unless he were a farmer
or a bacon curer. The fowl runs are elaborate aflairs,
and students are instructed oven in the making of hen
houses. ~ We inspected one large hen house; it was
made in sections by several students; each worked from
the same plan, and when each section was completed
the whole fitted exactly. There is not perhaps anything
very remarkable in this, but it indicates the thorough-
ness of the practical instruction given even in hen
house construction. There were hens of all kinds, big,
medium and small, and some of the hens lay as many
as about 170 eggs in the year. An ingenious but simple
contrivance enables the keeping of exact records of the
laying powers of each hen. This season about seven
hundred have, or will have, been reared, and these are
sold to the various poultry stations—or, at least, a large
proportion are sold, for only the best are purchased.
The various county committees are in close touch with
these various departments of live stock and poultry work
at the Model Farm, and they purchase cattle, pigs and
poultry here; the day before our visit'the Chairman of
the Cork County Council had been looking at some
animals. For the convenience of those in the West of
Ireland there are similar facilities afforded for purchas-
ing and precuring advice at Athenry and Ballyhaise, and
in the: South, to some extent, at Clonakilty. The
county committees are encouraged to take all the advan-
tage they can from these various stations.

Poultry: instructresses—and = domestic economy  in-
structresses also—are quite a large body in the State
just now; beyond the increasing need for them there is
another. reason why their ranks are in need of constant
recruits.  For their ranks are liable to decimation.
Start not, gentle matron, should you have decided on
this useful and nation-making career for your best
beloved daughter; it is not disease or the sword that
decifates the ranks. It is nothing so dreadful; it is
merely that the instructresses appear to have an alacrity
for getting married as Falstaff had for sinking. One
tough bachelor who had stood proof against all the
acknowledged wiles and fascinations of the sex surprised
all his, friends—indeed, he electrified a whole country-
side—by surrendering almost without a struggle to a
poultry instructress; his obdurate heavt softened, and
he was, conquered by a feather in the end.  We trust
that this little true story will not create too great a
run on the profession.

The residential capacity for students at the Model
Farm is from 50 to 60; at the present time there are
51 in residence. Thirty-six; of these are Albert or
Glasnevin College students, fifteen College of Science
students, and there are about eight practical gardeners
in training. The demand for the last-mentioned class
for instructing purposes is so great that the College has
to send some out before their term has really expired.
The Department awards ten agricultural scholarships
each year, tenable at the College of Science, and each
scholarship lasts for three years, so that there are
thirty scholarships running at any one time. Many of
the holders of these scholarghips reside in the Glasnevin
College, many others elect to live oufgide. There are
twenty-eight free places for the Glasnevin College itself

We saw a batch of students working in the fields; it
is part of the strenuous life unsuited to the premature
degenerates who take to ‘‘ the Bar ”’ or to ‘‘ med’cine,”
while their unfortunate papas and mayhap mamas
drudge behind the counter at home. Degenerates, pre-
mature or otherwise, are not wanted at the Model Farm.
Were Ircland in a normal social and industrial condi-
tion, particularly’ social, such places as the Glasnevin
Model Farm would loom in the eyes of the intelligent
youth of the country as does the British Civil Service
to-day. A few quiet hours spent in the class halls and
fields at the Glasnevin Farm clarifies the outlook on Ire-
land—it preocipitates green ingredients to the hottom.
Whatever the playgrounds of Eton may have been to
England, the fields and lahoratories and lecture halls of
such places as this are the training grounds for the real
battles where the destiny of Ireland must be decided.
Let who will discuss the relative merits of ‘ fighting the
battles of Treland”’ on ‘‘the floor of the ITouse’’ or
““the floor of the Tague,” it is at least certain that
well-directed energy expended on the floor of the stubble-
field and the floor of the fruit-garden brings forth
tangible results.

Tt is possible to increase the productivity of agricul-
tural Treland by means of good farming by very many
millions sterling a year. Iow is it that the idea of
making sacrifices for Ireland seldom appears to take the
shaps of constructive sacrifices? Ireland wants people to
sacrifice those of their traditional feelings and prejudices
and shibboleths that are in the way of the advance of
the country, face the realities of the situation, and
make sacrifices in the way of taking off their coats and
learning the science and practice of farming for the
good of themselves and the benefit of the nation.

We saw about a score of students busily engaged in
drawing up a scheme in connection with some phase of
applied agriculture; in the laboratory a number of
students were engaged in the work of chemical analysis;
two of them, by the way, were religious brothers, one
from Mount Bellew, Galway, and the other from Rams-
grange.

The fruit-gardens and vegetable plots are particularly
interesting portions of the farm. One plot of one-eighth
of an acre in extent inevitably brought the agricultural
labourers’ problem to mind. A cottage and an acre for
a labourer is only part of the solution of the problem ;
it might be called the political part, the other part of
the problem is—What is the labourer to do with the
acre when he gets it? The solution of this problem
leaves a wide field of activity open for many years to
the men being trained in the Department’s various
agricultural stations. Indeed, as well as the growing of
various vegetables there is the problem of eating them,
.:a.ud when you have got over the conservative prejudice
in favour of potatoes and cabbage for one period of the
year with a change to cabbage and potatoes during an-
other period—a big work in itself, for Irish prejudices
die ‘hard—you are face to face with the problem of
cooking them. Towever, we are not dealing | with
cookery now, though the Department is; but it is
another branch that we .must inquire into some other
time.  Fruit-growing has made comparatively great
trides in Treland in recent:years. One fact will indi-
cato this: within tho past four years about 700,000
fru ces. and  plants. have been  planted: in cottage
gardens in connection with the, work of the Depantment.

TFarming, for obvious reasons which it is quite unneces:
sary to touch upon, was, to a great extent, and,still is,
in Iroland a socially declassed industry. Like talking
Irish—it wasn’t supposed to be a, sign of respectability,
rather the reverse. "Weo have to chango all that absurd
state of public opinio We heard of a case where a
man spent £400 o ibes in order to secure for his son
a position as workhouse master at about £60 a year!
‘“Sure, ’twas cheap enough,” said ‘he, ““to make a
‘ gintleman * of him.”” * If 'he had spent one hundred
.pounds in making ia good farmer ofi hini,' the presump-
tion is that heicould never reach the social heights of
the workhouse master. Truly, we have'to learn more

Tearing up of a race of farmers who know the science
and practice of modern farming. The good unworked
land in Ireland is somewhat like its brother nation-
- killer—our ruinous railway system.
the railway nation-killers when we tackled them over
six years ago was that they could escape public attention
and public action because the manufacturers were despon-
dent, and the potential manufacturers were not aroused
to the possibilities of industry in this country. Stimu-
late an interest in manufacturing industry, and quite
automatically you produce a real combination and a
real force against the nation-killing rates and devious
ways of the railways in Ireland ; scatter Ireland all over
with men who know the potentialities of farming, and
the ranches will give way before the pressure as water
-goes down hill, or as the cheapest goods—class for class
—tend to capture the market. We think we are right
in asserting that many of the traditions of Irish farm-
ing have been lost in various parts of the country; but
even supposing the full volume of traditional farming
lore and skill were to the fore, the age of traditional
Irish farming is gone, that of the scientist, the calculator
-and the business man has succeeded, and the inexorable
law of the survival of the fittest in the world of industry
and commerce stands for a sentence of extinction on Ire-
land as a land of farms and farmers unless she faces
the realities of the situation.

IRELAND AT WORK.

A DUNDALK TOBACCO FACTORY.

W JAT with politics, fallible, or infallible as may
be alleged, conyentions, manifestoes, ‘‘Liberal’’
‘betrayals, counsels of wisdom and of desperation, a few
hours in a rising and prosperous Irish factory was a
delightful relief to us one day last week. Policies and
Governments come and go, the echoes of ‘“great
speeches ’’ die away, but the people go on day by day
eating, drinking, smoking, clothing and housing them-
selves, and the more material wealth Ireland realises
as a result of enterprise and labour the stronger she is
economically, whoever may be making ‘ telling points *’
in the debating halls or in the newspapers. From the
inevitable rancour of politics a few hours amid the con-
structive peace of industry, and a few hours discussing
industry from a purely industrial point of view, are in-
deed refreshing. What doth it profit a country if the
entire population rose to its feet, and cheered several
times as if they wero ‘ flinging back in their teeth’” a
Bill at a convention if the country were not able to
make even its own smoke?

A visit to a factory that more than doubled its output
and its hands within the past six years, and that is now
«cramped for room, and that is discussing plans—new
ground having already been purchased—for extending
itself is truly like music to our ears in this most dis-
tractful country. A tobacco factory, too! A factory
connected with an Irish industry that the Imperial
‘Combine was expected to wipe off the face of Ireland
with a sweep of its Imperial under-cut for green people.
But ‘“ Mick M‘Quaid Plug,” attended by its allies,
““ Premier ’ Brown Target, Dundalk Brown Roll, Anti-
Combine Plug, Travellers’ Joy Mixture, Rappahannoc,
Rich Flake, Golden Bar, wanted to know the reason
why, and, standing up to his fight, behaved with such
vigour and valour that our hearts soften even as we view
the woeful countenance of Terry Geraghty that adorns
the tins of Carroll’s ‘‘ Mick M‘Quaid Snuff.”

Monster factories like Gallagher’s and Goodbody’s
‘might have been counted upon to put up a good fight
against the Tmperial Combine ; but many may naturally
have had serious misgivings for moderate-sized factories,
like Carroll’s, of Dundalk. Five or six years ago Car-
roll’s had no account open in Scotland; to-day they
send about three tons a week of manufactured tobacco
there! The sturdy Irishmen there—influenced by the
LEeaper, as Mr. Carroll very generously admits—opened
the way to this big business that puts three tons weekly
of Irish-manufactured tobacco into the Scotch pipe!

We have not been in: every town in Ireland yet, but
Dundalk is the most prosperous-looking and ‘neatest
town of its kind that we have yet seen. Fortunately it
.does not appeal to one on account of the ‘‘ halo that
hovers round decay,”’ as, alas! so many other towns
appeal. It is largely a newly-built town—a town mak-
ing strides rather than desperately attempting to keep
on its feet. There are apparently quite an abundance

«of new houses both of the villa residence and the neat
workingman’s cottage type. Alas! that we should
have to say it, the ’98 memorial is probably some

-degrees less satisfactory than that at Thurles—and that

is saying a great deal. We hear that an attempt was

made to blow it up; and we understand that several
uncompromising’ Nationalists rather regret that the
attempt did not succeed! O, patriotism, what sculpture
is committed in thy name!

However, it is not with Dundalk, interesting and en-
couraging sight as it is to any Irishman who finds so

‘much backwardness and listlessness in other places, that

we arc specially concerned at present. But we
may remark—and some of the specially ‘‘drinky’’

‘towns may be interested in the fact—that the temper-

ance movement and temperance sentiment, are quite mili-
tant in. Dundalk. A drunken: man, we: learned, is a
rare sight in the town, and when he appears he is not
treated to any maudlin sympathy, or any ‘‘the poor
fellow, he took a drop too much.” The spirit of the
town is against him. It is only fair to say that the
Catholic clergy are largely responsible for this healthy
state of public feeling.

But let us return to our smoke. Carroll’s tobaceco
factory was started as far back as 1824. It has reached
the stage of being a medium-sized tree on 'the way to
being a great oak, and it commenced growing in 1824
from an acorn. It has, as we have said, more than
doubled its stalf and its traffic within the past five or six
years, and Mr. Carroll is sanguine that they will double
their present business in two or three years more. Want
of space hampers them for the present. Mr. Carroll
has recently acquired land in the Demesne, adjoining his
premises, for the purpose of extending his factory, and
has ‘also purchased the Demesne Flouse, formerly the
Dundalk residence of the Earls of Roden.  All this
sounds like Irish industry on the march; earls and
demesnes, pdg o’ beatac !

The preseng head of the firm, Mr. Vincent Carroll, son
of the founder of the industry, has three sons working in
the business, and there are reserves in the way of juve-
nile sons to be called on yet. So many Irish businesses
die out because the sons of the family go fox-hunting, or
become barristers without briefs, or doctors, in whose
hands the public do not care to risk their lives, it is
pleasant to find the third generation standing by the
industry. And Mr. Carroll’s sons work, and do not
merely play at work; the youngest in the business has
a taste for machinery, and, of course, there is a good
deal of machinery in a tobacco factory; we were intro-
duced to the young fellow, who emerged from the regions
of lubricating oil, as “a Clongowes boy who isn’t a
Seéomm.” Like a Trinity College Nationalist, we sus-
pect he is a sensible lad in spite of Clongowes. What
many of these colleges might have done for Irish pros-
perity and contentment had they Irish rather than
imitation English hearts!

Messrs. Carroll and Co. employ about 150 hands. The
clerical staff in Dundalk, and in the branch office in
Glasgow, are nearly all, if not all, Gaelic Leaguers. - If
some of the Irish Trelandersin Ireland were as practical,
in respect to such Irish manufactures, as some of them
are in Glasgow, there would be more Irish goods sold
in Ircland, though there might be less tall talk about
the cause. e

We have written so much already that we must lightly
skip over our remaining notes. We saw Irish tobacco—-
that is, tobacco grown in Ireland—in the factory. We
watched the making of ‘“ Emerald Gem cigarettes ;
the firm also make ““ Anti-Combine *’ cigarettes. The
manager, Mr. MacDonnell, is tobacco to the fourth
generation. His great grandfather was in the tobacco
industry in Ardee, his grandfather worked in various
tobacco factories in Dublin; and his father also worked
in Dublin and at Mountmellick where there was
then one of the oldest tobacco works in Tre.
land, so that if there is anything in the saying that
it takes three generations to make a Lancashire factory
}w.ud, one would conclude that if Mr. MacDonneil
is not an authority on tobacco there is nothing in
horedity. Mr. Carroll speaks very highly of the girls
in his employment, and of the interest they take in their
work, Ie would match them against the Belfast girls
any day, and be sure of their winning. However that
may be, we admire Mr. Carroll’s pride in Dundalk.
Unfortunately too many of us ‘are ready—perhaps not
always without some reason—to assert that our native
town, whatever it may be, is positively the worst and
most rotten town in Ireland. The Tmperial Combine
hn.ye forced Mr. Carroll out of the West. The West,
being a poor place, deals in the cheaper class of tobacco,
and it would not pay him to compete for it, so he has
practically withdrawn from Connacht. As we under-
stand it, even in the cheapest kind of roll tobacco the
consumer has to pay the same for the Combine stuff as
for Irish-manufactured roll competing with it, but the

Combine gives a cut to the retailer, and the retailer, in .- :

T

es, grabs at it;®the consumer pays the same,
“he only knew it, he is a fool not to insist on
getting Trish-made tobacco for Lis money. But who is
0 operate on the very poor consumers of the West, or
of other parts, for the matter of that—the very poor,
unintelligent people don’t read the Lraver at all. They
% could.be.l‘eached by the United Irish League if that
organisation had Irish industry in its heart, and did
not merely wear it in its resolutions and ¢ telling
speeches.””  Glasgow Irishmen have done splendidly by
this Dundalk industry, as, no doubt, by other Irish
industries also. How is it that Liverpool, Manchester
and London lag behind? Of course, London is a cum-
bersome place, but why don’t the Irish of Liverpool
B and Manchester use their various organisations as in-
5 struments for helping on Irish manufacture to the extent

WHAT CAN WE DG WITH OUR CAPITAL?

IN a recent issue your correspondent ‘‘ Ferdiad * ve-
quested suggestions for the investment of his sur-
plus money in Ireland, so as to give some one an opening
in life, *“ to increase industry and employment and thus
benefit the country.””

Being possessed of a scheme for the starting of a new
industry, and on the look for people with money to
invest, my heart leapt for joy on reading thus far. But
my joy was short-lived. The next sentence showed
that his patriotism was conditional on his being assured
of the ‘“ same interest, the same security, and the same
ease of realising ’’ his capital as if he had it invested in

Coats’.  Now, in the case of an industry about to be
‘“ started,’”” or one requiring  assistance,”” it is quite
obvious these are impossible conditions. It is no won-
der that, after due consideration, he rejects, as utterly
hopeless, every opening he can think of wherein an
TIrishman might invest money in his own country. This
attitude of mind is so typical of the average Irish
capitalist, that if your correspondent is merely playing
a réle, he must be said to assume the character to per-
fection. He would like to do good with his money ;
looking at his decaying country, his conscience pricks
him; and to ease his mind and justify his apathy, he

tries to show that there is no hope of success for new

enterprises, and that to talk of curing the country’s
ills by such means is only indulging a new form of

ravineis. 3

In support of this view ‘‘Ferdiad’’ contends (1)
that no small manufacture can be run with profit, at
least, by a Company ; (2) that even with a sufficiency of

- ‘capital, it would be nonsense for a body of country

shopkeepers and farmers and professional men to com-

- bine to start an industry at which they are all amateurs;
'(3) that if they did so combine, they could not get a
good manager, except for a salary which the moderate
trade of the new factory could not possibly pay him.

Recent experience proves these three statements to be

-wholly fallacious. Have we not read in the papers of

several new factories having been established here and

there during the past few years by the formation of

Companies—an example of which I would quote the

Sligo Shirt Factory? There are other instances of the

success attendant on native eriterprise, so ‘niumerous and

~ so striking as to completely refute the assertions of

-your pessimistic correspondent, not to Be found amongst

the monied classes, the shopkeepers and professional

men, but amongst the class on whom ‘ Ferdiad '’ casts

2 most unjust aspersion, namely, the farmers. Did he

over seo or hear of a Co-operative Society for the manu-
acture of centrifugal creamery butter? 'For' all the

pg;p‘ ses of our argument such a Society is a Llm:hed

i ibqzt Company. Surely the humble and, in most

_poor farmers composing it were amateurs at the
they were entering on, as the product they
ng out required the highest technical skill
